frameworks.name: planning model of train/recruit decisions
frameworks.one_line: agents pick collaborators to maximize long-run returns minus hiring and training costs

frameworks.name: myopic decision models
frameworks.one_line: choose by current competence or single-step improvement, ignoring downstream task success

frameworks.name: intuitive theory of competence
frameworks.one_line: lay reasoning that competence is plastic and improves with task-specific practice

core_question: How do people decide whom to train or recruit for a collaborative task given that collaborators' competence is plastic?
key_assumptions: competence is not static and improves with task-specific practice
collaboration entails costs of hiring and training that must be weighed against returns
decision-makers can represent both current expertise and trainability of others
manipulations: training opportunities available on the job
decision type (whom to train vs. whom to recruit)
key_findings: participants' choices were best captured by a planning model balancing collaboration returns against hiring/training costs
planning model outperformed myopic alternatives based on current strength or single-step improvability
decisions reflected neither pure current-competence selection nor pure improvability selection
interpretations: people use an intuitive theory of competence to plan over future returns when assembling collaborators
joint reasoning about plasticity and cost underlies team-formation decisions
